Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to set aside evenings after work, but with limited resources and a lot going on, I ended up studying whenever I could grab a moment. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. Some weeks I'd manage 19 hours, other weeks barely anything. The structure I wanted just never really materialized the way I planned.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I'm 27, so I've got responsibilities that younger students might not have—bills, work, that kind of thing. When deadlines loomed, I'd cram hard, but during quieter weeks, I'd let things slide. Regular study times were more of an aspiration than a reality for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification about new content that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important since my attendance wasn't great. The anxiety of falling behind was actually a big motivator—I'd feel this surge of panic and suddenly I'm on the LMS at midnight trying to catch up.
Q4: For a brief period mid-semester, I did get into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It became almost automatic for a few weeks. But then life got complicated—work picked up, money stress increased—and that routine fell apart. I kept meaning to get back to it, but I never quite did.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me. Being an auditory learner, I could absorb so much more by listening than by reading. I watched nearly 300 minutes of video content because that's genuinely how I learn best. The discussion boards were less useful to me personally, but I appreciated when peers explained concepts in different ways.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With my limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading on screen for hours gave me headaches. I also delayed the discussion posts because they felt awkward to me—I'm not great at putting my thoughts in writing under pressure.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to assignments or exams. The videos that broke down complex topics into manageable chunks—those were gold. If something felt like busywork or didn't clearly help me understand the material better, I'd deprioritize it, especially when time was tight.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're stretched thin and anxious about the required stuff, the optional content becomes this guilt-inducing presence. I knew they might help, but required activities always took priority. There was definitely a mental weight difference—required felt urgent, optional felt aspirational.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them repeatedly on my phone during my commute and at work during breaks. I also found some YouTube channels that explained similar concepts, which I used extensively. My actual learning happened a lot outside the LMS—the platform was more like a starting point than my whole study environment.
Q10: There were many times I'd be listening to downloaded lectures while walking or working, genuinely engaging with the material, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also discuss concepts with a coworker who'd taken similar courses, working through problems verbally. That kind of auditory processing and conversation-based learning doesn't leave any digital trace.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video once, download the audio or take voice notes summarizing it, and then replay those recordings dozens of times. The LMS would show one view, but I might have engaged with that content for hours across multiple days. It was more efficient for me to work offline given my schedule.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My anxiety spiked to the point where just opening the LMS made my heart race. I was also dealing with financial stress—low resources meant choosing between buying materials or other necessities. The combination of high anxiety and external pressures made engagement feel almost impossible some weeks.
Q13: Massively. Fatigue was a constant companion—I'd log into the LMS exhausted after work and just stare at the screen. Confusion about complex topics without adequate support made me avoid certain modules entirely. When work demanded overtime or unexpected expenses hit, the course became secondary to survival. The LMS usage definitely reflected those competing demands.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered to financial worries or work problems. I'd scroll through materials without really processing anything, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something. The anxiety made it hard to focus even when I was technically present.
Q15: I postponed the longer assignments constantly, even knowing my assignment completion rate was suffering. The anxiety made starting feel overwhelming—the bigger the task, the more I'd avoid it. I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace, but that perfect moment rarely came. It's frustrating because when I did complete assignments, I scored well.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense anxiety spikes, but they were also the only thing that forced me to actually submit work. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the 24-48 hours before something was due. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less, honestly.
Q17: The CBT session I had actually helped me reframe how I thought about feedback. Before that, negative feedback would spiral me into avoidance. After the intervention, I tried to see feedback as information rather than judgment. My mood improved, and I started checking feedback more regularly, though implementing changes was still challenging.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a bit overwhelming for someone in my situation. Twenty online courses is a lot to manage, and the way content was organized didn't always match how I needed to learn. I wished there were more audio-focused options built in. The structure assumed a level of consistent availability I just couldn't maintain.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier—that was well-designed for someone like me. But the heavy reliance on written discussion posts and text-based materials made things harder. The peer interaction features were good when I used them, but navigating everything with high anxiety meant I often felt lost in the platform's complexity.
Q20: I think that's probably someone like me—high anxiety, genuinely trying, but struggling to convert engagement into completed work. You might be researching, rewatching videos, trying to understand, but the actual submission feels like this massive hurdle. The gap between understanding and producing something feels enormous when you're anxious and overwhelmed.
Q21: That could be someone who's learned to be efficient or who studies heavily offline like I do. My exam score doesn't reflect how much I actually engaged with the material through listening and external resources. Some people just don't need to be visibly active in the LMS to be learning—they've found what works for them elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, time spent. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I'm anxiously scrolling versus deeply processing. They miss all the offline learning, the conversations, the repeated listening to downloaded content. They capture presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material, not just being exposed to it. It's when I'm listening to a lecture and suddenly something clicks, or when I'm explaining a concept to someone and realize I understand it. It's active mental participation, which can happen anywhere—not just logged into a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my genuine effort or the barriers I faced. It wouldn't show the hours of audio learning during commutes, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or how the CBT session changed my perspective. It wouldn't capture that my high assignment scores when I did submit show I understood the material—I just couldn't consistently show up due to circumstances beyond what any log could measure.
